name: Lawrence Brown
abstract: Lawrence Brown (August 3, 1907 – September 5, 1988) was an American jazz trombonist from California best remembered for his work with the Duke Ellington orchestra. He was a session musician throughout his career, and also recorded albums under his own name.

concerts.label: Duke Ellington in His Concert of Sacred Music

quotes.text: she was with, you know, and then he came with us, finally. And I remember distinctly one of his terrific things he did was on a transitional phrase, I think it was, "Black, Brown and Beige." Which he played all by himself, in no time, you know, just played and it was so terrific that at the concert in Carnegie Hall the audience gave him applause. Really applauded, very definitely, for this passage. It was beautiful, beautiful. And all through the years he did such terrific work and then he finally came with Johnny and he really helped the band there, very much.
quotes.caption: Brown recalls audience applause for a soloist during Black, Brown and Beige

about_quotes.speaker: Jean Bach
about_quotes.evidence: that’s how I’ve met my dream prince of all time, that I’ve never gotten over, Lawrence Brown. And he was my favorite. And so I sat next to him and we talked.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jean Bach interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Schuller Gunther
about_quotes.evidence: He came down off the stage, and he approached me... He came down, and we talked a little bit.
about_quotes.doc_title: Schuller Gunther — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: Lawrence Brown on trombone... One of the outstanding things was on that date was Lawrence Brown's trombone playing behind the side 'Morning Glory' -- behind Joe Turner when he sang

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: we had Lawrence Brown, you know it was just great having these people... Duke was on one of his periodic European trips where he didn’t take the band and I got to use them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I was playing piano, with Lawrence Brown, Johnny Hodges, Al Hall the bass player, Denzil Best the drummer, and Billy Strayhorn was playing congas.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: Lawrence Brown came here -- of course, he came here and the first thing he did was get sick. And he played with me.
